### 4. Dynamic element Render `talkTitle` inside a heading whose tag comes from `headingLevel`. ```html ${talkTitle} ```
### 5. Dynamic attribute Render a link to `website` with the text `Speaker page`. *Bonus: what happens to `href` if `website` were empty?* ```html Speaker page ``` An empty/null value removes the `href` entirely — no `href=""` leftover.
## Part B — Options
### 6. Context / escaping Render `bioHtml` twice: once so the bold/italic format, once so the tags show as literal text. ```html
${bioHtml @ context='html'}
${bioHtml @ context='text'}
```
### 7. Join Render the `tags` on one line as `ai · math · history`. ```html ${tags @ join=' · '} ```
### 8. Format with a number Render `Talk length: 45 min` using `durationMinutes`. ```html ${'Talk length: {0} min' @ format=[durationMinutes]} ```
### 9. i18n Render a button whose label `Register now` goes through the translation dictionary. ```html ``` With no dictionary it simply prints the original string.
## Part C — Loops, reuse, comments
### 10. List + itemList Render `agenda` as an ordered list (`
    `), appending ` ← last` to the final item only. ```html
    1. ${step} ← last
    ``` HTL has no arithmetic, so `index + 1` is impossible — `
      ` numbers for you.
### 11. Template + call Define a reusable `pill` template and use it for every tag. ```html ```
### 12. Comments Add one developer note that must **not** reach the browser, and one comment that **does**. ```html ```
## Challenge
### 13. One-line summary In a single expression, render `Ada Lovelace — Weaving Algorithms (45 min) [KEYNOTE]` (the `[KEYNOTE]` part empty when not a keynote). ```html ${'{0} {1} — {2} ({3} min) {4}' @ format=[ firstName, lastName, talkTitle, durationMinutes, isKeynote ? '[KEYNOTE]' : '' ]} ```
--- ## Advanced — Part D Real data is rows of records, but **HTL has no inline map/object literal** — so a list of records is modelled with **parallel arrays read by the loop index**, a pattern you meet when data arrives pre-shaped from Java. ### Advanced GIVEN — paste this for Part D ```html ```
### 14. Records via parallel arrays Render a `` with one row per session: title, speaker, room — lined up by loop index. ```html
${title} ${sessionSpeakers[titleList.index]} ${sessionRooms[titleList.index]}
``` Alternative: `` repeats the row itself.
### 15. Dynamic class Give the row whose index equals `featuredIndex` the class `featured` (no empty `class=""` otherwise). ```html
${title}
```
### 16. Condition inside a loop In each row show the duration and append ` — LONG` only for sessions longer than 45 minutes. ```html
${title} ${sessionMinutes[titleList.index]} min — LONG
```
### 17. Template with computed params Define a `row` template taking `title, speaker, room, featured`, then call it once per session — computing `featured` as you call it. ```html
```
### 18. Conditional unwrap Render every session title, but wrap **only the featured** one in ``. Use `data-sly-unwrap`. ```html ${title} ```
### 19. The limit Render the price as `42.50 EUR` from `priceCents` (= 4250). Try it — then explain *why pure HTL can't*. **Not possible in pure HTL.** There is no division (4250 / 100) — HTL has no arithmetic at all. Formatting money belongs in a Sling Model. ```html ${model.displayPrice} ${currency} ``` `@ format` can format a number but cannot divide it.
### 20. Nested loops Render an outline: each `outlineSections` entry as a heading, followed by its matching `outlineItems` sub-array as a `
    `. ```html

    ${section}

    • ${item}
     ``` Relies on nested array literals — works on HTL 1.4+ (AEM 6.4+/Cloud). On older instances, return the nested list from a Sling Model.

title: How a Web Page Renders
text:
Before writing HTML, it helps to picture what the browser actually does with it — from your request
to pixels on screen.

## 1 · The request

![A browser window sending a GET request to a server](img/html-render/1-request.png)

Your browser sends a **GET** request to the server, asking for the page.

## 2 · The response

![The server returning an HTML document back to the browser](img/html-render/2-response.png)

The server responds with the **HTML document** — just text.

## 3 · The HTML

![An HTML document with head, title, a linked stylesheet, and a body with a heading and list](img/html-render/3-html.png)

That HTML describes the page's **structure and content**: a heading, a list, and a link to the CSS.

## 4 · The DOM

![The HTML parsed into a DOM tree of html, head, body, and child nodes](img/html-render/4-dom.png)

The browser parses the HTML into the **DOM** — a tree of nodes that it (and your JavaScript) work with.

## 5 · The CSS

![A CSS file setting background, font-size, and colors](img/html-render/5-css.png)

It also fetches the **CSS** the HTML referenced.

## 6 · The CSSOM

![The CSS parsed into a CSSOM tree of style rules attached to elements](img/html-render/6-cssom.png)

CSS is parsed into the **CSSOM** — a matching tree of style rules.

## 7 · The render tree

![The DOM and CSSOM combined into a render tree of visible, styled nodes](img/html-render/7-render-tree.png)

The **DOM + CSSOM** combine into the **render tree** — only what's visible — which the browser then
lays out and paints to the screen.

<Callout type="note">HTML is step one of all of this. The rest of the track is about writing that HTML well — structured, semantic, and accessible.</Callout>

title: HTML Fundamentals
text:
HTML is the **structure and content** layer of the web. CSS adds presentation, JavaScript adds behaviour — but HTML comes first, and a page works (and is accessible) on HTML alone. {/* Commented out for now — this "behind the scenes" page-load journey will be handled in the PowerPoint slides instead. Kept here (and the component) in case we want to bring it back into the lesson later. ## First, what actually happens when a page loads You write HTML every day — but the browser does a surprising amount behind the scenes between "request" and "pixels on screen". Click through each stage below: the Did you know? note on each is something most developers have never had to think about, yet it explains a lot of everyday behaviour (why scripts block, why CSS goes in the head, why the DOM never quite matches your file). Keep these stages in mind as you go through the track — almost every "best practice" later (semantic markup, script placement, lazy-loading, avoiding layout thrash) is really about being kind to one of these steps. */} ## The minimal HTML5 document ```html My Page

Hello, world

``` ```mermaid flowchart TD D["DOCTYPE html"] --> H["html element — lang"] H --> HEAD["head — metadata"] H --> BODY["body — visible content"] HEAD --> M["meta charset utf-8"] HEAD --> V["meta viewport"] HEAD --> T["title"] ``` ## The five things every document needs - **``** — must be the very first line. It switches the browser into modern ("standards") mode. It is **not** an HTML tag — just a required declaration. - **``** — the root element. The `lang` attribute tells browsers, screen readers, and search engines the page language (affects pronunciation, hyphenation, translation). - **``** — the character encoding. Always UTF-8, and put it **first** in the `` so the browser decodes the rest correctly (accents, emoji, symbols). - **``** — makes the page responsive on mobile; without it, phones render at a zoomed-out desktop width. - **``** — the tab/bookmark label and a major SEO signal. ## head vs body - **`<head>`** — metadata *about* the page: title, encoding, viewport, links to CSS, scripts. Nothing here is visible in the page itself. - **`<body>`** — everything the user sees and interacts with. <Callout type="do">Start every project from this exact skeleton. The `charset`, `viewport`, and `lang` lines are not optional boilerplate — each one fixes a real rendering or accessibility problem.</Callout> <Callout type="note">HTML5 is forgiving — browsers will render messy markup — but "it renders" isn't "it's correct". The rest of this track is about writing HTML that is structured, accessible, and valid.</Callout>

title: The DOM & Attributes
text:
A browser parses your HTML into a **tree of nodes** — the **DOM** (Document Object Model). Every tag becomes an *element node*; text becomes *text nodes*; attributes hang off element nodes. CSS selects nodes, and JavaScript reads and changes them. ```mermaid flowchart TD DOC["document"] --> HTML["html"] HTML --> HEAD["head"] HTML --> BODY["body"] BODY --> H1["h1 — element node"] BODY --> P["p — element node"] H1 --> T1["'Welcome' — text node"] P --> A["a — element node"] A --> T2["'Read more' — text node"] ``` ## Anatomy of an element ```html Read more ``` - **Tag**: `a` (opening `` … closing ``) - **Attributes**: `href="/docs"`, `class="link"` — always `name="value"`, value quoted - **Content**: the text/children between the tags Nesting creates parent → child → sibling relationships. **Close tags in the order you opened them** — overlapping tags (``) are invalid. ## Void (empty) elements Some elements have **no content and no closing tag** — they're self-contained: ```html
``` ## Global attributes (work on any element) | Attribute | Purpose | |---|---| | `id` | unique identifier (one per page) | | `class` | one or more class names (styling/JS hooks) | | `title` | advisory tooltip text | | `lang` | language of this element's content | | `hidden` | hides the element | | `tabindex` | keyboard focus order | | `style` | inline CSS (use sparingly) | | `data-*` | your own custom data | ## Boolean attributes Present = true, absent = false. Their value doesn't matter — just being there enables them: ```html ``` ## `data-*` — your own attributes Attach custom data to an element without abusing `class` or `id`. Read it in CSS (`[data-state="open"]`) or JS (`el.dataset.state`): ```html ``` In AEM front-ends this is exactly how the component loader works — `data-nc="MyComponent"` and `data-nc-params-…` carry behaviour and config to JavaScript. (See the JavaScript track.) ## Block vs inline (the old mental model) Historically elements were "block" (start on a new line, take full width — `div`, `p`, `section`) or "inline" (flow within text — `span`, `a`, `strong`). CSS can override this with `display`, but the **default content model** still guides what may nest where (e.g. you can't put a `
` inside a `

`). Pick elements for their **meaning**, then style with CSS. The DOM you produce is what assistive tech, search engines, and your JavaScript all read.

title: Semantic HTML & Document Outline
text:
**Semantic** HTML uses elements for their *meaning*, not their appearance. It's the single highest-leverage habit in front-end: you get accessibility, SEO, and maintainability for free. ## Landmark elements ```mermaid flowchart TD B["body"] --> HE["header — banner"] B --> N["nav — navigation"] B --> M["main — main content"] B --> F["footer — contentinfo"] M --> ART["article"] M --> AS["aside — complementary"] ``` | Element | Use for | |---|---| | `
` | introductory content / page or section masthead | | `
` | a block of navigation links | | `
` | the primary content — **one per page** | | `
` | a self-contained, independently distributable unit (blog post, card, comment) | | `
` | a thematic grouping, usually with a heading | | `
` | tangential content (sidebars, callouts, related links) | | `
` | closing content (copyright, links) for a page or section | ```html
…site logo + masthead…
…primary menu…

Article title

A part

…
Related links
© 2026
``` "Div soup" — everything is a `
`, meaning is carried only by class names: `
` `

`. Screen readers and crawlers see nothing but boxes. Use the landmark that matches the role. They become **navigable landmarks** for screen-reader users and are understood by search engines out of the box. Edit the markup — the same semantic regions, lightly styled so you can see the structure: Header / banner
Nav: Home · Guides · Shop

Article title

The main content lives here.

Related links
© 2026
`} css={`header, nav, main, article, aside, footer { padding: .6rem .8rem; margin: .3rem 0; border-radius: 6px; } header { background: #6b2fb3; color: #fff; } nav { background: #ece6f6; } main { display: flex; gap: .5rem; } article{ background: #f7f7fb; flex: 1; } aside { background: #fbf3da; width: 32%; } footer { background: #1c1c28; color: #fff; }`} /> ## Heading hierarchy (h1–h6) Headings form the document **outline** — the table of contents assistive tech and search engines build. Rules: - **One `

```

Storage
A clean outline: one title, then nested sub-topics — no skipped levels. Try the **headingsMap** browser extension or your dev-tools accessibility tree to *see* your outline. If it doesn't read like a sensible table of contents, the structure is wrong.

title: Text & Links
text:
## Text-level semantics Inline elements carry meaning, not just style. Pick by intent: Toggle each example between its **source** and the **rendered result**: | Element | Means | Example (toggle code / result) | |---|---|---| | `` | strong importance | | | `` | stressed emphasis | | | `` | highlighted / relevant | | | `` | machine-readable date/time | | | `` | abbreviation + expansion | | | `` | inline code | | | `` | keyboard input | | | `` / `` | subscript / superscript | | | `
` + `` | quotation + source | | ```html

Ship it by June 30. This is critical — see the RFC.

```

Ship it by June 30. This is critical — see the RFC.

 Use ``/`` for *meaning* (a screen reader emphasises them); use ``/`` only for styling with no added importance. In practice you'll rarely need ``/``. ## Links — the `` element ```html Relative link (same site) Absolute link Jump to a fragment (an id on the page) Email us Call us Download the PDF ``` - **Relative** (`/docs`, `../img.png`) stays within your site; **absolute** (`https://…`) points anywhere. - **Fragment** links (`#id`) scroll to the element with that `id` — the basis of in-page nav and "skip to content" links. ## Opening in a new tab — safely ```html Opens in new tab ``` When you use `target="_blank"`, add `rel="noopener noreferrer"`. Without `noopener`, the new page can access `window.opener` and redirect your page (a phishing vector). Modern browsers do this by default now, but be explicit. Link text should make sense on its own — screen-reader users often jump link-to-link. Avoid "click here"; write "read the install guide".

title: Forms
text:
Forms collect user input. A good form is **labelled, validated, and submittable** — and most of
that is built into HTML.

## The form element

```html
<form action="/subscribe" method="post">
  …controls…
  <button type="submit">Subscribe</button>
</form>
```

- **`action`** — where the data goes. **`method`** — how:
  - **GET** appends data to the URL (search, bookmarkable, non-sensitive).
  - **POST** sends data in the request body (changes state, larger/sensitive data).
- For file uploads add `enctype="multipart/form-data"`.

## Labels — always

Every control needs a label, associated by `for` ↔ `id` (or by wrapping):

```html
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

<!-- or wrap it -->
<label>Email <input type="email" name="email"></label>
```

<Callout type="warn">A `placeholder` is **not** a label — it disappears on typing and fails accessibility. Always provide a real `<label>`.</Callout>

## Common inputs

The `type` attribute changes the keyboard, validation, and UI:

```html
<input type="text">      <input type="email">    <input type="password">
<input type="number">    <input type="tel">      <input type="url">
<input type="date">      <input type="file">     <input type="range">
<input type="checkbox">  <input type="radio">    <input type="color">
<input type="hidden">
```

Plus the multi-line and choice controls:

```html
<textarea name="message" rows="4"></textarea>

<select name="country">
  <optgroup label="EU">
    <option value="be">Belgium</option>
    <option value="de">Germany</option>
  </optgroup>
</select>
```

Build a form live — try different input `type`s, then submit it empty to see native validation:

<Playground
  html={`<form>
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input id="email" type="email" required placeholder="you@example.com">

  <label for="size">Size</label>
  <select id="size">
    <option>Small</option>
    <option>Large</option>
  </select>

  <label class="check"><input type="checkbox"> Subscribe</label>

  <button type="submit">Sign up</button>
</form>`}
  css={`form { display: grid; gap: .6rem; max-width: 320px; }
label { font-weight: 600; }
.check { font-weight: 400; }
input, select, button { padding: .5rem; font: inherit; }
button { background: #6b2fb3; color: #fff; border: 0; border-radius: 6px; cursor: pointer; }`}
/>

## Grouping & built-in validation

```html
<fieldset>
  <legend>Account</legend>

  <label for="user">Username</label>
  <input id="user" name="user" required minlength="3">

  <label for="age">Age</label>
  <input id="age" name="age" type="number" min="18" max="120">

  <label for="site">Website</label>
  <input id="site" name="site" type="url" pattern="https://.*">
</fieldset>
```

- **`<fieldset>` + `<legend>`** group related controls (essential for radio/checkbox sets).
- **Validation attributes** — `required`, `minlength`/`maxlength`, `min`/`max`, `pattern`, and the
  `type` itself — let the browser validate before submit, **no JavaScript needed**.

<Callout type="do">Lean on native validation and input `type`s first. They give you mobile keyboards, date pickers, and error messages for free, and they work without JS.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">`<button>` defaults to `type="submit"` inside a form. Use `type="button"` for JS-only buttons and `type="reset"` to clear — being explicit avoids accidental submits.</Callout>

title: Media & Graphics
text: ## Images ```html ``` 🔗 **Try it on W3Schools:** [HTML Images](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_images.asp) - **`alt`** is required — describe the image, or use `alt=""` for purely decorative images. - **`width`/`height`** let the browser reserve space → no layout shift (CLS) while loading. - **`loading="lazy"`** defers off-screen images for performance. ## Responsive images Serve the right size/format for each device. **`srcset` + `sizes`** — same image, multiple resolutions; the browser picks: ```html ``` **``** — *art direction* (different crops) or *format fallback* (modern formats with a safe fallback): ```html ``` 🔗 **Try it on W3Schools:** [Responsive images](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_responsive.asp) · [Picture element](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_images_picture.asp) Always keep a plain `` inside `` — it's the fallback and the element that actually carries `alt`. ## Audio & video ```html ``` 🔗 **Try it on W3Schools:** [HTML Video](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_video.asp) · [HTML Audio](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_audio.asp) - Multiple ``s let the browser choose a supported format. - **``** adds captions/subtitles — important for accessibility. - `poster` shows a placeholder image before playback. ## Inline SVG SVG is vector graphics in markup — sharp at any size, styleable with CSS, scriptable: ```html ``` Edit the SVG — change the shapes, `viewBox`, or `fill`, and watch it redraw: SVG `} css={`svg { background: #f7f7fb; border-radius: 8px; }`} /> Use **inline SVG** when you need to style/animate parts or use `currentColor`; use **``** for simple, cacheable icons you don't need to manipulate.

title: Embedded Content (iframe)
text: An **` ``` 🔗 **Try it on W3Schools:** [HTML Iframes](https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_iframe.asp) ## The attributes that matter | Attribute | Why | |---|---| | **`title`** | **Required for accessibility** — screen readers announce it as the frame's name | | `src` | the embedded document's URL | | `width` / `height` | reserve space (or size with CSS) | | `loading="lazy"` | defer off-screen iframes — they're heavy | | `sandbox` | restrict what the embedded page can do (scripts, forms, popups…) | | `allow` | feature permissions (`allow="fullscreen; autoplay"`) | | `referrerpolicy` | control what referrer info is sent | ## Security & performance An iframe loads a **whole other page** — it's heavy and a security surface. Only embed sources you trust, and tighten it with `sandbox` (start with nothing allowed, then add the minimum, e.g. `sandbox="allow-scripts"`). Note that `allow-scripts` + `allow-same-origin` together can let the frame remove its own sandbox. Prefer a native element when one exists — `

title: Metadata, SEO & the <head>
text:
The `<head>` is invisible but decides how your page is **indexed, shared, and loaded**.

## Core metadata

```html
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Espresso Guide — Acme Coffee</title>
  <meta name="description" content="A practical guide to pulling great espresso at home.">
  <meta name="robots" content="index, follow">
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://acme.example/guides/espresso">
</head>
```

What each one does:

- **`<meta charset="utf-8">`** — the page's character encoding. Put it **first** in the `<head>` so the browser decodes accents, symbols, and emoji correctly.
- **`<meta name="viewport">`** — makes the layout responsive; without it, phones render the page at a zoomed-out desktop width.
- **`<title>`** — the browser-tab/bookmark label **and** the bold clickable line in a search result. Make it unique per page (~50–60 chars).
- **`<meta name="description">`** — the grey snippet under the title in search results. Not a ranking signal, but it drives click-through, so write it for humans (~150 chars).
- **`<meta name="robots">`** — instructs crawlers: `index, follow` (the default), `noindex` (keep this page out of results), `nofollow` (don't pass link credit from it).
- **`<link rel="canonical">`** — names the *real* URL when the same content is reachable via several (tracking params, http/https, trailing slash), so search engines consolidate them instead of treating them as duplicates.

## Social sharing — Open Graph & Twitter

What shows when the page is pasted into Slack, LinkedIn, X, etc.:

```html
<meta property="og:title" content="Espresso Guide">
<meta property="og:description" content="Pull great espresso at home.">
<meta property="og:image" content="https://acme.example/og/espresso.png">
<meta property="og:type" content="article">
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
```

## Structured data (schema.org / JSON-LD)

Machine-readable facts that power rich results (ratings, breadcrumbs, FAQs):

```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "Espresso Guide",
  "author": { "@type": "Person", "name": "Ada Lovelace" },
  "datePublished": "2026-06-05"
}
</script>
```

Real cases you'll actually ship:

**Product** — price + rating earn a star rich result:

```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Product",
  "name": "Acme Espresso Machine",
  "image": "https://acme.example/img/machine.jpg",
  "offers": {
    "@type": "Offer",
    "price": "499.00",
    "priceCurrency": "EUR",
    "availability": "https://schema.org/InStock"
  },
  "aggregateRating": { "@type": "AggregateRating", "ratingValue": "4.6", "reviewCount": "128" }
}
</script>
```

**Breadcrumbs** — the path shown under the title in results:

```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "BreadcrumbList",
  "itemListElement": [
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 1, "name": "Guides", "item": "https://acme.example/guides" },
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 2, "name": "Espresso", "item": "https://acme.example/guides/espresso" }
  ]
}
</script>
```

<Callout type="note">Validate with Google's [Rich Results Test](https://search.google.com/test/rich-results) — it shows which rich result a page qualifies for and flags missing required fields. Keep the JSON-LD facts **in sync with the visible page**, or search engines ignore (or penalise) it.</Callout>

## Resource hints & script loading

```html
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.example">
<link rel="preload" href="/hero.avif" as="image">
<script src="/app.js" defer></script>
```

- **`preconnect`/`dns-prefetch`** warm up connections to third-party origins.
- **`preload`** fetches a critical asset early.
- **`defer`** (run after HTML parses, in order) and **`async`** (run as soon as ready) keep scripts
  from blocking rendering. Prefer `defer` for app scripts.

How each one affects HTML parsing:

![A plain script blocks parsing while it downloads and executes; async pauses parsing to execute as soon as it has downloaded; defer downloads in parallel and executes only after parsing completes](img/html/script-loading.svg)

- **`<script>`** (no attribute) — parsing **stops**, the script downloads, runs, *then* parsing resumes. Render-blocking.
- **`<script async>`** — downloads alongside parsing, but **pauses** the parser to run the moment it arrives (order not guaranteed). Good for independent third-party scripts (analytics).
- **`<script defer>`** — downloads alongside parsing and runs **after** the HTML is fully parsed, **in document order**. The safe default for your app scripts.

<Callout type="do">Minimum viable `<head>`: charset, viewport, title, description. Add canonical + Open Graph for anything public-facing, and JSON-LD where rich results matter.</Callout>

title: Standards, Validation & Best Practices
text: The finish line: writing HTML that is **valid, conventional, compatible, and fast** — then putting the whole track together in one document. ## Validate Run your markup through the **[W3C validator](https://validator.w3.org/)**. It catches unclosed tags, duplicate `id`s, misnested elements, and invalid attributes — bugs that cause subtle, cross-browser rendering and accessibility problems. ## Coding conventions - Lowercase tags and attributes; always quote attribute values. - One UTF-8 encoding, declared first. - Indent to show nesting; one block-level element per line. - Meaningful `class` names (the project uses **BEM** — see the CSS track). - Don't rely on the browser auto-fixing sloppy markup. ## Browser compatibility - Check support on **[caniuse.com](https://caniuse.com/)** before using a newer feature. - **Progressive enhancement**: start with HTML that works everywhere, then layer on CSS and JS. The content is readable even if CSS/JS fail. - **Graceful degradation / fallbacks**: `` fallback ``, `

title: JavaScript Basics
text:
JavaScript adds interactivity to a website. The language core is compact; on top of it sit browser APIs, third-party APIs, and frameworks. ## Variables & constants Declare with `let` or `const` (avoid `var`). Assign, then optionally reassign. ```js let name = 'John'; // declare + assign name = 'Bob'; // ok const id = 42; // can't be reassigned // id = 7; // TypeError const list = []; // the binding is constant, the contents aren't list.push('hello'); // ['hello'] ← fine ``` Default to `const`; use `let` only when you must reassign. Never `var`. ## Scope - **Global** — accessible everywhere - **Function** — visible only inside the function - **Block** — `let`/`const` inside `{ }` (if, for, while) are visible only in that block **Lexical scope:** an inner function can read variables from its enclosing scope. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph G["Global scope"] subgraph F["Function scope"] subgraph B["Block scope — if / for / while"] V["let / const declared here
visible only inside the block"] end end end ``` Inner scopes can read outward; outer scopes cannot read in. ```js const name = 'John'; function outer() { const surname = 'Wick'; // inner can see name + surname return `${name} ${surname}`; } // surname is NOT visible out here ``` ## Data types `String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Array`, and `Object` (functions, DOM nodes — everything else — are objects). ## Operators ```js 6 + 9; // 15 (also string concat: 'a' + 'b') 20 / 2; // 10 let n = 4; n === 3; // false (strict equality) n !== 3; // true ``` ## Control flow & loops ```js if (food === 'ice cream') { /* … */ } else { /* … */ } for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) { /* runs 5 times */ } ``` Also: `switch`, `while`, `do…while`, `for…of` (values), `for…in` (keys), and `return` / `break` / `continue`. ## Functions ```js function sum(a, b) { return a + b; } sum(3, 5); // 8 ``` ## Classes & `this` A **class** is a blueprint for creating objects. Each object you build from it is an **instance**. Use a class when you have several things that share the same shape and behaviour — like every component on a page. ```js class Greeter { // The constructor runs once, when you create an instance with `new`. // It sets up the instance's starting data (its "state"). constructor(name) { this.name = name; // store a property ON this instance } // A method — a function that belongs to the class. greet() { console.log(`Hello ${this.name}`); } } const g = new Greeter('World'); // `new` builds an instance and runs the constructor g.greet(); // Hello World ``` Three words to be clear on: - **`new`** — creates a fresh, empty instance and runs the `constructor`. - **property** (`this.name`) — a piece of data stored on the instance. **method** (`greet`) — a function the instance can run. - **`this`** — inside the class, `this` refers to **the specific instance you're working with**. Each instance has its own. ### `this` is per-instance Make two instances and `this.name` is different in each — the methods are shared, the data is not: ```js const a = new Greeter('Ada'); const b = new Greeter('Linus'); a.greet(); // Hello Ada b.greet(); // Hello Linus ``` ### The classic `this` gotcha When you pass a method as a callback, it can **lose its `this`** — inside `greet`, `this` is no longer the instance: ```js button.addEventListener('click', g.greet); // ❌ `this` is lost button.addEventListener('click', () => g.greet()); // ✅ arrow keeps the surrounding `this` button.addEventListener('click', g.greet.bind(g)); // ✅ bind locks `this` to g ``` Passing `obj.method` as a callback detaches it from `obj`. Wrap it in an **arrow function** (`() => obj.method()`) or use **`.bind(obj)`** so `this` still points at the instance. This is exactly how the components in the next lessons work: each one is a **class**, and the loader creates an **instance** per element with `new MyComponent(element, options)` — so `this.element` is *that* element, and every component keeps its own state. ## Events (a first look) ```js const button = document.querySelector('.button'); button.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('clicked')); ``` Run it yourself — edit the JS and watch the console pane update: Output appears in the console below 👇

`} js={`function sum(a, b) { return a + b; } console.log('sum(3, 5) =', sum(3, 5)); class Greeter { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } greet() { return 'Hello ' + this.name; } } console.log(new Greeter('World').greet());`} /> These fundamentals power everything in the next lessons — especially classes + events, which the Netcentric component pattern is built on.

title: Modern JavaScript
text:
The most useful recent additions to the language — each shown **before → after** so the win is clear. ## Optional chaining `?.` Short-circuits to `undefined` instead of throwing when part of the chain is missing. **Before** ```js const city = person && person.address && person.address.city; ``` **After** ```js const city = person?.address?.city; onChange?.(); // call only if it exists ``` Reading data that might be missing — API responses, optional config, optional callbacks. ## Logical assignment `||=` `??=` Assign only when a condition holds — great for defaults and lazy init. **Before** ```js if (!opts.mode) opts.mode = 'auto'; this.cache = this.cache || new Map(); ``` **After** ```js opts.mode ||= 'auto'; // set if falsy this.cache ??= new Map(); // set only if null/undefined ``` Apply a default, or lazy-init a value the first time it's needed. ## Dynamic import & top-level await Load code only when it's actually needed, and `await` directly at module top level. **Before** ```js import Chart from './chart.js'; // always in the bundle ``` **After** ```js const { default: Chart } = await import('./chart.js'); // loaded on demand ``` Defer a heavy or rarely-used dependency out of the initial bundle — a key performance lever. ## Promise helpers **Before** ```js const [a, b] = await Promise.all([p1, p2]); // rejects if either one fails ``` **After** ```js await Promise.allSettled([p1, p2]); // every result, success or failure await Promise.any([p1, p2]); // first success wins ``` `allSettled` when you need **every** result even if some fail; `any` for **first success wins** (e.g. fallback endpoints). ## Arrays & strings **Before** ```js arr[arr.length - 1]; // last item [...arr].sort(); // copy first — .sort() mutates the original ``` **After** ```js arr.at(-1); // last item arr.toSorted(); // sorted copy, original untouched str.replaceAll('+', ' '); // every occurrence, no global regex ``` `.at(-1)` for the last item; the immutable `toSorted` / `toReversed` / `with` when you must not mutate shared state. ## ES modules **Before (CommonJS)** ```js const { add } = require('./math.js'); module.exports = { add }; ``` **After (ES modules)** ```js import add, { name } from './math.js'; export const name = 'Ada'; // named export export default function add(a, b) {} // default export ``` Named exports for a set of utilities; a default export for a module's one main thing; re-exports to build a barrel (`index.js`). Tweak the values and watch the console: Open the console below 👇

`} js={`const user = { name: 'Ada' }; // no 'age' property console.log(user?.address?.city); // undefined — no error console.log(user.age ?? 18); // 18 console.log([3, 1, 2].toSorted()); // [1, 2, 3] console.log([10, 20, 30].at(-1)); // 30`} /> `?.` removes most defensive `if (x && x.y)` checks. Reach for `toSorted` / `with` when you must not mutate shared data.

title: JS Components & the Component Loader
text:
In Netcentric/AEM projects, JavaScript is initialised through the **Component Loader** — an open-source library that wires DOM elements to JS classes. You don't call your component yourself; you **register** it, mark up an element, and the loader creates an instance for you. **Read along in real code:** the `assignment/js-starter-accordion` branch of the `code` project has a complete, annotated **Accordion**. Get it with `git fetch && git checkout assignment/js-starter-accordion`, then open `ui.frontend/.../publish/components/accordion/accordion.clientlibs.js` — every `[NN]` comment maps to a lesson. This one is `[03]` (`register` + the `data-nc` hook in `accordion.html`). ## What it does - Scans the DOM for elements that declare a component (via a `data-nc` attribute). - Creates a **class instance** for each match, passing the element + options. - Keeps watching the DOM and initialises **new** elements added later — essential for a WYSIWYG CMS like AEM, where authors add components live. ## What you need to wire up a component For the loader to pick up and run your component, **five things** must line up. Miss one and nothing happens (no error) — so it's worth knowing them all. 1. **A class that accepts `(element, options)`.** The loader calls `new YourClass(element, options)` for every match. Keep the constructor tiny: store them and call `init()`. 2. **Register the class** with `register({ MyComponent })`. The **name you register** (`MyComponent`) is the exact name the loader will look for in the HTML. 3. **Make sure the build includes it** — export it from the publish entry (`index.js`) so the bundler ships it. Code that's never imported never runs. (See *Shipping Component JS*.) 4. **Mark up the element** with `data-nc="MyComponent"`. The value must match the registered name **exactly** (it's case-sensitive). 5. **The loader runtime must run on the page** — `run()` + `observe()`, loaded via the publish JS bundle. Without it, nothing initialises at all. Optional, but common: 6. **Pass configuration** with `data-nc-params-MyComponent='{ … }'` (valid JSON). It arrives in the constructor as `options` — covered in the next lesson. ## Registering a component ```js import { register } from '@netcentric/component-loader'; class MyComponent { constructor(element, options) { this.element = element; // the DOM node with data-nc="MyComponent" this.options = options; // parsed from data-nc-params-… this.init(); } init() { // do something with this.element … } } register({ MyComponent }); ``` ## Wiring it from HTML The loader matches the registered name to elements carrying the `data-nc` attribute: ```html
…
``` On `DOMContentLoaded` the loader checks every registered component against the DOM; on a match, the class is instantiated. ## The lifecycle, end to end ```mermaid flowchart TD H["HTML: data-nc = MyComponent"] -->|loader scans DOM
and watches for new nodes| N["new MyComponent(element, options)"] N -->|constructor stores refs| I["init() — your behaviour starts"] ``` Keep the constructor tiny — store `element`/`options`, then call `init()`. Put real setup (queries, listeners) in `init()` so the flow is easy to read. ## Why isn't my component running? When a component silently does nothing (and there's **no error**), it's almost always one of the five requirements above: - the `data-nc` value **doesn't match** the registered name (typo or wrong case); - the class is **never imported/exported**, so the build left it out; - the **publish JS bundle isn't loaded** on the page, so the loader never runs; - the `data-nc-params-…` value is **not valid JSON** (single quotes, trailing comma) and fails to parse. A mismatch produces **no error** — the loader just finds nothing to do. When in doubt, walk the checklist below: it's nearly always a name mismatch or a missing bundle. ## Quick checklist - [ ] A class that takes `(element, options)` and does setup in `init()` - [ ] `register({ MyComponent })` - [ ] Exported from the publish entry so the build bundles it - [ ] `data-nc="MyComponent"` on the element — name matches **exactly** - [ ] Publish JS bundle (loader `run()` / `observe()`) loaded on the page - [ ] *(optional)* config via `data-nc-params-MyComponent='{ … }'` as valid JSON The loader is open source — you can read and contribute to it. Next we'll pass data in (parameters) and find inner elements (DOM references).

title: Passing Parameters & DOM References
text:
A component usually needs two things from the page: **configuration** (parameters) and access to its **inner elements** (DOM references). **In the example** (`assignment/js-starter-accordion`): `accordion.clientlibs.js` `[04]` — `this.options` from `data-nc-params`, and queries scoped to `this.element`. ## Passing parameters Pass data from HTML to JS with a `data-nc-params-` attribute holding **valid JSON**. The loader parses it and hands it to the constructor as `options`. ```html
``` ```js class MyComponent { constructor(element, options) { this.element = element; this.options = options; // { text: "Hello from params" } this.init(); } init() { console.log(this.options.text); // "Hello from params" } } ``` The params attribute must be **well-formed JSON** (double-quoted keys/strings). A trailing comma or single quotes will fail to parse. This is the bridge from the **back end / authoring** to your JS: anything HTL can output, you can pass into the component. ## DOM references `this.element` is the component's root node (the one with `data-nc`). Find inner elements relative to it — **not** with document-wide queries. ```js init() { this.title = this.element.querySelector('.my-component__title'); this.items = this.element.querySelectorAll('.my-component__item'); } ``` Always scope queries to `this.element`. A page may contain many instances of the same component — `document.querySelector('.title')` would grab the wrong one. ## 🛠 Practice Read a `text` param and write it into `.my-component__text`: ```html
``` ```js init() { const el = this.element.querySelector('.my-component__text'); if (el) el.innerHTML = this.options.text; } ``` The loader parses the param and your code writes it into the empty span:

Hello My Component (from params)

title: Events & Event Delegation
Custom Events
Shipping Component JS
text:
You've written a component class and registered it. This lesson is how that JS actually gets **built, bundled, and booted** on a page in this project. **In the example** (`assignment/js-starter-accordion`): `accordion.clientlibs.js` `[07]` + `accordion.html` — the clientlib category that loads the component's CSS/JS. ## The end-to-end flow ```mermaid flowchart LR J["my-component.clientlibs.js
register MyComponent"] --> I["components/index.js
exports it"] I --> B["nc-fe-build / webpack
bundle + minify"] B --> C["clientlib
academy.publish"] C --> H["loaded from HTL on the page"] ``` ## Where component JS lives Mirroring the SCSS layout, JS sits next to its component in `ui.frontend`: ```text ui.frontend/src/main/frontend/academy/publish/components/my-component/ ├── my-component.clientlibs.js └── my-component.clientlibs.scss ``` ## Register, then export The component **registers itself** with the loader, and is **exported** from the publish entry so the bundler includes it: ```js // my-component.clientlibs.js import { register } from '@netcentric/component-loader'; class MyComponent { constructor(element, options) { this.element = element; this.options = options; this.init(); } init() { /* … */ } } register({ MyComponent }); export default MyComponent; ``` ```js // publish/components/index.js — make the build aware of it export * from 'academy-base-project/publish/components/my-component/my-component'; ``` ## The build `nc-fe-build` (webpack) compiles and minifies the JS into the publish clientlib (e.g. `publish.bundle.js`) under a category like `academy.publish`. ```bash npm run build # full build (styles + webpack) npm run watch:js # rebuild JS on change while developing ``` ## Loading it from HTL The page/component pulls the JS clientlib the same way it pulls CSS: ```html ``` (`clientlib.js` = scripts only, `clientlib.css` = styles only, `clientlib.all` = both.) Load the publish JS bundle once (usually from the page template's footer libs), not per component — the loader then wires up every `data-nc` element on the page, including ones authors add live. This parallels the Sass track's "Styling an AEM Component" — same `ui.frontend` → build → clientlib → component flow, for behaviour instead of styling.

title: Author Mode & Re-initialization
text:
AEM is a **WYSIWYG** CMS: authors add, edit, move, and delete components **live** on the page — so
your component's JS runs in situations a plain website never sees. The component loader is built for
exactly this, through two calls.

## `run()` vs `observe()`

The loader boots **once per page** from the publish entry:

```js
// publish.clientlibs.js
import { run, observe } from '@netcentric/component-loader';

run();      // initialise every component already in the DOM (on page load)
observe();  // keep watching the DOM and initialise components added/re-rendered later
```

- **`run()`** scans the current DOM **once** and instantiates each `data-nc` component it finds.
- **`observe()`** keeps watching (via a `MutationObserver`). When AEM **re-renders** a component
  after an author edits its dialog — or an author **drops a new one** — `observe()` instantiates it
  with no page reload.

<Callout type="note">On a published page `run()` alone would do. **`observe()` is what makes author mode work**, because the DOM keeps changing as the author edits.</Callout>

## Make `init()` idempotent

Because a component can be initialised **more than once** (a re-render, a re-insert), binding a
listener in `init()` without a guard **stacks duplicates** — one click then fires twice, three
times… Guard it so the second init bails out:

```js
init() {
  if (this.element.dataset.ncReady) return;   // already initialised — stop
  this.element.dataset.ncReady = 'true';

  // safe to query and bind listeners now
}
```

<Callout type="warn">No guard = the classic author-mode bug: every re-init adds another listener, so the handler runs multiple times per click. Idempotent `init()` is the fix.</Callout>

## Run only on the published page

Some behaviour shouldn't run in the editor (autoplay, tracking, animations). Skip it in author mode:

```js
if (wcmmode === 'edit') return;
```

<Callout type="note">**In the example** (`assignment/js-starter-accordion`): `accordion.clientlibs.js` `[09]` — the idempotent `init()` guard that survives author re-render.</Callout>

title: Performance Patterns
JS Best Practices for AEM Components
text: The habits that make component JS robust on author-composed pages — a checklist for everything in this track. **In the example** (`assignment/js-starter-accordion`): the whole `accordion.clientlibs.js` is these habits in practice — `[11]` exports the class for unit testing; scoping, the `data-*` contract, and no globals run throughout. ## Scope to `this.element` `document.querySelector('.button')` — grabs the first match anywhere, so it hits the wrong instance when a page has several of your component. `this.element.querySelector('.button')` — only ever touch your own subtree. Many instances coexist safely. ## Progressive enhancement The HTML should be useful **before** JS runs (and if it fails). JS *enhances*; it isn't the only way to get content. ```html Details ``` Render real content/links in HTL; use JS to upgrade behaviour. Provide `

```

**Your task:** it already *dispatches* an `accordion:toggle` custom event (`[06]`) that nothing listens
to yet. Make accordions **coordinate** — when a panel opens in one accordion, close any open panels in
the *other* accordions on the page, by listening to that event (not by reaching across components).

- [ ] Put a second Accordion on the page (author it, or add a node under `…/root/responsivegrid`)

title: Assignment: Build a Component
text:
Build **one real component — a Dropdown** — applying the track's patterns step by step. Each step uses one lesson; do them in order. Build it in `ui.frontend`. Budget **~1.5 hours**. **Starter project:** check out the `assignment/js-starter-dropdown` branch — the component (HTL, dialog, SCSS) and a demo page are all wired up; only `dropdown.clientlibs.js` is left as **TODOs**. Deploy it, then fill in that one file — no scaffolding. Stuck? Compare with the [answer](assignment-answer). Start from markup that already works without JS (a button + a list). Your JS only *enhances* it — that's progressive enhancement. The markup you enhance: ```html
  • One
  • Two
``` ## Step 1 — Register the class (lesson 03) - [ ] A class taking `(element, options)` — store them, then call `init()` - [ ] `register({ Dropdown })` so the loader creates one for every `data-nc="Dropdown"` ## Step 2 — Params & DOM refs (lesson 04) - [ ] Read `open` from `this.options` (the parsed `data-nc-params`) - [ ] Find the toggle button and the menu with `this.element.querySelector(…)` — scoped, never `document` ## Step 3 — Wire the event (lesson 05) - [ ] On toggle click, show/hide `.dropdown__menu` (its `hidden` attribute) and keep `aria-expanded` in sync - [ ] Close it when a click lands **outside** `this.element` ## Step 4 — Announce changes (lesson 06) - [ ] Dispatch a `dropdown:toggle` custom event (with `{ open }` in `detail`) so other components can react ## Step 5 — Survive author mode (lesson 08) - [ ] Guard `init()` so running it twice never binds a second listener (idempotent) ## Step 6 — Ship it (lesson 07) - [ ] Export the class from the publish entry so the build bundles it and the loader runs it Done when: the button toggles its own menu, two Dropdowns on a page work independently, it fires `dropdown:toggle`, and `init()` is safe to run twice. Best practice (lesson 10): scoped to `this.element`, no globals, works without JS. Stretch (lesson 09): debounce a `resize`/`input` handler if your component needs one. Prefer a real AEM component? See [AEM: Extend the Accordion](aem-assignment).

title: Assignment — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](assignment) first. The habits that matter: scope to `this.element`, idempotent `init()`, and communicate via a custom event.</Callout>

```js
// publish/components/dropdown/dropdown.clientlibs.js
import { register } from '@netcentric/component-loader';

class Dropdown {
  constructor(element, options) {                 // [03] the loader calls this
    this.element = element;
    this.open = Boolean(options && options.open);  // [04] read a param
    this.init();
  }

  init() {
    if (this.element.dataset.ncReady) return;       // [08] idempotent — safe to run twice
    this.element.dataset.ncReady = 'true';

    // [04] DOM refs scoped to this.element
    this.toggle = this.element.querySelector('.dropdown__toggle');
    this.menu = this.element.querySelector('.dropdown__menu');
    this.setOpen(this.open);

    // [05] events
    this.toggle.addEventListener('click', () => this.setOpen(!this.open));
    document.addEventListener('click', (event) => {
      if (!this.element.contains(event.target)) this.setOpen(false);  // close on outside click
    });
  }

  setOpen(open) {
    this.open = open;
    this.menu.hidden = !open;
    this.toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(open));
    // [06] announce the change — anyone can listen, no direct references
    this.element.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('dropdown:toggle', {
      bubbles: true,
      detail: { open }
    }));
  }
}

register({ Dropdown });   // [07] shipped via the publish bundle
export default Dropdown;  // exported so it can be unit-tested
```

<Callout type="do">Two Dropdowns coexist (scoped refs), it survives a double `init()`, and it announces every change via `dropdown:toggle` — without knowing who's listening.</Callout>

title: What is a Preprocessor
text:
We don't write CSS to deliver CSS — we write **SCSS** and let a build step compile it. A **preprocessor** gives us a more comfortable syntax with variables, nesting, functions and mixins, then outputs plain CSS the browser understands. ## Why bother? Plain CSS has real limits at scale: - lots of **repetition** - **no reusability** of logic - **no real logic** (conditionals, loops) - limited **flexibility** Modern front-end deals with complexity through architecture (BEM, ITCSS, OOCSS), design systems — and preprocessors are one of the tools that make that practical. ## Where it fits in the build ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SCSS you write"] -->|preprocess / compile| B["CSS"] B -->|postprocess| C["Optimised CSS
autoprefixed + minified"] C --> D["Shipped to the browser"] ``` In this project that's `nc-fe-build`: it compiles `*.scss` into clientlib CSS, then post-processes (autoprefixing, etc.). ## SCSS vs Sass syntax There are two syntaxes. **SCSS** is a superset of CSS (curly braces and semicolons — every valid CSS file is valid SCSS), and it's what we use: ```scss $brand: #6b2fb3; .button { background: $brand; &:hover { filter: brightness(1.1); } } ``` Because SCSS is a superset of CSS, you can paste existing CSS straight in and improve it incrementally — no rewrite needed. ## Trying it quickly The [Sass playground](https://sass-lang.com/playground/) compiles SCSS → CSS live in the browser — handy for experimenting with the features in the next lessons. Preprocessor power is a means, not an end. The output is still CSS — keep it lean, and don't let nesting or clever functions hide what the final CSS actually does.

title: Values & Operators
Nesting & the & selector
Functions & Mixins
Styling an AEM Component
text:
In AEM, a component's CSS is delivered as a **client library** (clientlib). In this project you author **SCSS** in `ui.frontend`, the build compiles it into a clientlib under a **category**, and the component loads that category from its HTL. ## The end-to-end flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S["SCSS
ui.frontend/.../teaser.clientlibs.scss"] --> B["nc-fe-build
compile + minify"] B --> C["clientlib
category: academy.publish.components.teaser"] C --> H["teaser.html
data-sly-call clientlib.all"] H --> P["styled component on the page"] ``` ## Where the SCSS lives ```text ui.frontend/src/main/frontend/academy/publish/components/teaser/ └── teaser.clientlibs.scss ``` The build derives the **category from the folder path**: `academy/publish/components/teaser` → `academy.publish.components.teaser`. ## Write the styles (the project pattern) The project uses a `$module-name` variable + BEM (enforced by stylelint — next lesson): ```scss @use "variables/colors" as *; $module-name: "teaser"; .#{$module-name} { &__title { font-size: 2rem; color: var(--brand); } &__cta { text-decoration: underline; } &--muted { opacity: .7; } } ``` ## Load it from the component's HTL Pull the category in with the Granite `clientlib` template: ```html

${teaser.title}

``` - `clientlib.all` injects CSS **and** JS; `clientlib.css` / `clientlib.js` load just one. - The `categories` string must match the generated category **exactly**. One SCSS folder per component, named to match the component, producing one predictable category. That keeps the clientlib graph obvious as the project grows. This mirrors the Component Creation track's "Adding the Clientlibs" lesson — here we focus on the **SCSS authoring** side; there on the component wiring.

title: Project Style Architecture
BEM in AEM Components
text:
This project styles components with **BEM** (Block · Element · Modifier), enforced by stylelint. BEM keeps selectors flat (single classes), so styles are easy to override on purpose and hard to break by accident — which matters when authors compose many components on a page. ## The convention ```text .teaser Block — the component root .teaser__title Element — a part of the block .teaser--featured Modifier — a variant of the block .teaser__title--big Element modifier ``` ## The `$module-name` pattern Components define the block name once and build selectors with `&`: ```scss $module-name: "teaser"; .#{$module-name} { display: grid; &__title { font-size: 2rem; } // .teaser__title &__cta { text-decoration: underline; } // .teaser__cta &--featured { // .teaser--featured .#{$module-name}__title { color: var(--brand); } } } ``` Rename the component once (`$module-name`) and every selector follows. ## Match the markup The HTL carries the same classes, so markup and styles line up one-to-one: ```html

${teaser.title}

Read more
``` ## Keep specificity flat Deeply nested selectors (`.teaser .body .title a span`) — high specificity, hard to override, brittle when an author reorders components. One class per element. Nest at most ~2 levels, and only to scope a modifier (`.teaser--featured .teaser__title`). The output selector should stay a single class wherever possible. ## Stylelint enforces it The project's stylelint config requires kebab-case BEM class names and flags violations in the build: ```js // stylelint.config.js (project) 'selector-class-pattern': '^[a-z]([a-z0-9-]+)?(__([a-z0-9-]+))?(--([a-z0-9-]+))?$' ``` A class that doesn't match the BEM pattern fails lint. Stick to `block__element--modifier`, all lowercase, kebab-case — no camelCase, no IDs for styling. ## It powers the Style System AEM's **Style System** lets authors pick visual variants from the component toolbar — and under the hood, those variants are just **BEM modifier classes**. The flow: 1. You define the modifier in SCSS **once** — `.teaser--featured { … }`. 2. The template policy gets a `cq:styleGroups` entry for that variant: a label (*"Featured"*) mapped to the class `teaser--featured`. 3. In the page, the author picks **Featured** from the component's toolbar. 4. AEM adds `teaser--featured` to the component's element — and the SCSS you already wrote styles it. A new author-selectable variant is usually **no new code**: one more BEM modifier in your SCSS, plus one entry in the policy. (Policies & the Style System are covered in the Components track.)

title: CSS Best Practices for AEM
text:
Pages are composed from many independently-authored components. These practices keep one
component's CSS from breaking another's — and keep pages fast.

## Scope styles to the component

<Callout type="dont">Bare element selectors leak across the whole page: `h2 { color: red; }`, `ul { margin: 0; }`, `a { … }`. Any other component's `h2`/`ul`/`a` is now affected.</Callout>
<Callout type="do">Namespace everything under the component's block class: `.teaser h2` or, better, `.teaser__title`. A component should only ever style **its own** subtree.</Callout>

## Keep specificity low and flat

- One class per element (BEM). Avoid IDs and long descendant chains.
- Never reach for `!important` to win a fight — fix the selector or use a modifier class. (If you
  must override a library, prefer **cascade layers** — see the CSS track.)

## Manage clientlib relationships

- **`dependencies`** — categories that must load **before** this one (e.g. a base/atomic lib).
- **`embed`** — inline another category's output **into** this one (fewer requests; use deliberately).

```text
# a component clientlib that needs the atomic colors first
dependencies = [academy.publish.atomic.colors]
```

<Callout type="do">Use `dependencies` for shared bases so order is guaranteed; reserve `embed` for combining a few small libs into one request. Don't embed large shared libs into every component — you'd duplicate them.</Callout>

## Performance (ties into the Web Performance track)

- Ship only what a page needs — per-component clientlibs beat one giant global stylesheet.
- CSS is **render-blocking**: keep it lean, minified (the build does this), and consider
  **critical CSS** for above-the-fold content.
- Style media with `width`/`height` or `aspect-ratio` to avoid layout shift (CLS).
- Prefer animating `transform`/`opacity` (compositor-friendly) over layout properties.

## Theming & variants

- Expose brand/spacing as **CSS custom properties** so themes and the **Style System** can override
  them live, without recompiling.
- Author-selectable variants = BEM **modifier** classes toggled by `cq:styleGroups` (Components
  track) — no per-variant code.

## Responsive

- Mobile-first: base styles for small screens, layer up with `min-width` media queries using the
  **shared breakpoints** (`common/styles`).
- Lean on intrinsic sizing (Flexbox/Grid wrapping, `min()`/`max()`/`clamp()`) so a component adapts
  to the space it's given with as few media queries as possible.

## Quick checklist

<Callout type="do">Before you commit a component's SCSS, check: scoped to the block class · BEM, low specificity · no `!important` · uses shared tokens, not hard-coded values · author-only styles behind `wcmmode` · media sized to avoid CLS · passes stylelint.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">Together with the Components track (clientlibs, policies, Style System) and the Web Performance track (critical CSS, fonts, images), this is the full picture of styling AEM components well.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Architect & Apply SCSS
text:
Pick up the SCSS you wrote in the [mid-module checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) and turn it into a
**maintainable architecture** — partials, `@use`/`@forward`, token maps — then apply it the way you
would on a real AEM component. Applies lessons **05–09**. Budget **~2 hours**. Build it in your
project's `ui.frontend` SCSS.

<Callout type="note">Extend the same SCSS — don't restart. If you skipped the checkpoint, do [Part 1](checkpoint-assignment) first (variables, nesting, functions, mixins); everything here organizes and applies those.</Callout>

## Part 1 — Architecture (Intermediate)

- [ ] Split into **partials**: `_variables.scss`, `_mixins.scss`, component partials
- [ ] Wire them with **`@use` / `@forward`** (no `@import`) — namespaced where it helps
- [ ] Put your tokens in a **map** (e.g. `$spacing: (sm: .5rem, md: 1rem, lg: 2rem)`) and read with `map.get`
- [ ] Generate a spacing/utility set with **`@each`**
- [ ] A **`@mixin`** breakpoint helper driven by your breakpoints map

**Acceptance:** one entry point (`_index.scss`) `@forward`s the shared layer; components `@use` tokens instead of redefining them; no `@import`; changing a token ripples everywhere.

## Part 2 — Apply it to an AEM component (Advanced)

- [ ] Author **one component's styles as its own partial** (`hello.clientlibs.scss`-style), `@use`-ing the shared tokens/mixins — never redefining them
- [ ] Use **BEM-in-AEM** naming so the compiled selectors are flat, single-class, and namespaced to the component
- [ ] Add an **author-mode guard** for any edit-only styling (e.g. styles applied only under `.cq-Editable-dom` / behind a `wcmmode` wrapper)
- [ ] Sanity-check the **compiled output** is lean — no deep selector chains, no duplicated declarations

**Acceptance:** the component partial reuses the shared layer (no copy-pasted tokens); selectors stay flat BEM; author-only styling doesn't leak to publish.

## Stretch goals

- A typography mixin (`font-size` + `line-height` from a scale).
- Organise files ITCSS-style (settings → tools → components).
- Add a `map`-driven theme and switch it with `@each`.

<Callout type="do">Done when: a single change (a token, a mixin) ripples through the whole stylesheet, the file structure reads clearly, the component partial reuses the shared layer, and the compiled CSS is no bigger than your hand-written version.</Callout>

title: Assignment — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](assignment) first. The goal is one source of truth: tokens in a map, an `_index.scss` that `@forward`s the shared layer, and components that `@use` it rather than redefine it.</Callout>

## File structure

```text
common/styles/
├── _tokens.scss     ← variables + maps
├── _mixins.scss     ← button(), bp() …
└── index.scss       ← @forward barrel (the entry point)
publish/components/card/
└── card.clientlibs.scss
```

## The shared layer

```scss
// _tokens.scss
$brand: #6b2fb3;
$spacing: (sm: .5rem, md: 1rem, lg: 2rem);
$breakpoints: (md: 48rem, lg: 64rem);
```

```scss
// _mixins.scss
@use "sass:map";
@use "tokens" as *;

@mixin bp($name) {
  @media (min-width: map.get($breakpoints, $name)) { @content; }
}

@mixin button($bg, $fg: #fff) {
  padding: map.get($spacing, sm) map.get($spacing, md);
  border: 0; border-radius: 6px;
  background: $bg; color: $fg; cursor: pointer;
}
```

```scss
// index.scss — the entry point: @forward passes the partials along
@forward "tokens";
@forward "mixins";
```

## Generate utilities with `@each`

```scss
@use "tokens" as *;
@each $name, $val in $spacing {
  .p-#{$name} { padding: $val; }     // .p-sm / .p-md / .p-lg
}
```

## Apply it on a component (flat BEM, author-guarded)

```scss
// card.clientlibs.scss
@use "../../../common/styles" as *;   // the _index entry point — tokens + mixins in one

.card {
  padding: 1rem;
  @include bp(md) { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 120px 1fr; }
}
.card__cta { @include button($brand); }

// edit-only styling, behind author mode
.cq-Editable-dom .card { outline: 1px dashed $brand; }
```

<Callout type="do">Change a token or a mixin once and every consumer updates; the compiled CSS stays flat single-class BEM, and author-only rules never reach publish.</Callout>

title: Checkpoint: SCSS Language Basics
Checkpoint — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">Attempt the [checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) first. This covers the SCSS language basics; the [final assignment](assignment) splits it into a maintainable architecture.</Callout>

```scss
@use "sass:math";

// variables — one source of truth
$brand: #6b2fb3;
$radius: 6px;
$space: 1rem;

// @function — returns a value, emits no CSS
@function rem($px, $base: 16px) {
  @return math.div($px, $base) * 1rem;
}

// @mixin — emits CSS, with default args and an @if decision
@mixin button($bg, $fg: #fff) {
  display: inline-flex;
  padding: math.div($space, 2) $space;
  border: 0;
  border-radius: $radius;
  background: $bg;
  color: $fg;
  cursor: pointer;

  @if $bg == transparent {
    border: 1px solid $brand;   // the ghost variant needs an outline
  }

  &:hover { filter: brightness(1.08); }
}

// nesting + & for BEM, the mixin reused for two variants
.btn {
  font-size: rem(16px);
  @include button($brand);

  &--ghost { @include button(transparent, $brand); }
}
```

<Callout type="do">Change `$brand` or the `button` mixin and every consumer updates. Nesting stays ≤ 2 levels, so the compiled output is flat, single-class BEM — no copy-pasted blocks.</Callout>

title: Testing Fundamentals
text:
A **unit test** checks one small piece of code (a function, a module) in **isolation**, automatically.
On the front end it lets you change code with confidence — refactor, add features, fix bugs — and
get an instant signal if you broke something.

## Why bother?

- **Confidence** to refactor and ship — the tests catch regressions.
- **Faster feedback** than clicking through the UI by hand.
- **Living documentation** — a test shows how a function is meant to be used.
- **Better design** — code that's hard to test is usually too tightly coupled.

## The testing pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  E["End-to-end — few, slow, full app in a browser"]
  I["Integration — some, modules working together"]
  U["Unit — many, fast, one piece in isolation"]
  E --> I --> U
```

This track is about the **base of the pyramid**: fast, focused unit tests (with a little DOM
testing) using **Jest**.

## The AAA pattern

Almost every good test has three parts:

```js
test('adds two numbers', () => {
  // Arrange — set up inputs and state
  const a = 2, b = 3;

  // Act — run the thing under test
  const result = sum(a, b);

  // Assert — check the outcome
  expect(result).toBe(5);
});
```

## What makes a good unit test

<Callout type="do">A good test is **fast**, **isolated**, **deterministic**, and tests **behaviour/output** — not private implementation details. If a behaviour-preserving refactor breaks it, the test was too tightly coupled.</Callout>

title: Jest Basics
Matchers
text: A **matcher** is the assertion after `expect(...)`. Picking the right one makes failures readable. ## Values: `toBe` vs `toEqual` ```js expect(2 + 2).toBe(4); // Object.is — primitives & same reference expect({ a: 1 }).toEqual({ a: 1 }); // deep (recursive) equality — use for objects/arrays expect({ a: 1, b: undefined }).toStrictEqual({ a: 1 }); // strict: also checks undefined props/type ``` `toBe` compares **identity**, so `expect({a:1}).toBe({a:1})` **fails** — two different objects. Use `toEqual` for objects and arrays. ## Numbers & strings ```js expect(value).toBeGreaterThan(3); expect(value).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3.5); expect(0.1 + 0.2).toBeCloseTo(0.3); // floats — don't use toBe expect('Netcentric').toMatch(/centric/); // regex / substring ``` ## Arrays & objects ```js expect(['a', 'b', 'c']).toContain('b'); expect([{ id: 1 }]).toContainEqual({ id: 1 }); // deep contain expect(list).toHaveLength(3); expect(user).toHaveProperty('address.city', 'Berlin'); expect(user).toMatchObject({ name: 'Ada' }); // subset match ``` ## Exceptions ```js expect(() => parse('')).toThrow(); // throws anything expect(() => parse('')).toThrow('empty input'); // message contains expect(() => parse('')).toThrow(ValidationError); // error type ``` Pass a **function** to `expect` when testing throws — expect(parse('')).toThrow() would run `parse` *before* expect sees it, so the error escapes the test. ## DOM matchers (jest-dom) The `@testing-library/jest-dom` package adds expressive matchers for DOM nodes (more in the DOM testing lesson): ```js expect(button).toBeInTheDocument(); expect(button).toBeDisabled(); expect(el).toHaveTextContent('Subscribe'); expect(el).toHaveClass('is-active'); expect(input).toHaveValue('hello'); ``` Choose the **most specific** matcher — `toHaveLength(3)` fails with a clearer message than `toBe(3)` on `list.length`, and `toBeInTheDocument()` reads better than `not.toBeNull()`.

title: Mocking
Async Testing
DOM Testing
Test Organization
Coverage & Quality
text: The last piece: knowing **how much** of your code is tested, and making sure those tests are **meaningful** — not just there to hit a number. ## Measuring coverage ```bash npx jest --coverage ``` Jest reports four metrics: | Metric | Means | |---|---| | **Statements** | % of statements executed | | **Branches** | % of `if`/`else`/ternary/`&&` paths taken | | **Functions** | % of functions called | | **Lines** | % of lines executed | **Branches** is the most revealing — it's easy to run a line without testing both sides of its conditions. ## Enforcing a threshold Fail the build if coverage drops below a bar (in `jest.config.js`): ```js export default { collectCoverage: true, coverageThreshold: { global: { branches: 80, functions: 80, lines: 80, statements: 80 } } }; ``` ## Coverage is a guide, not a goal 100% coverage does **not** mean bug-free. A test can *execute* a line without asserting anything meaningful about it. Coverage tells you what's **not** tested; it can't tell you whether what *is* tested is tested **well**. ```js // 100% coverage, 0% value — no assertion about behaviour test('renders', () => { render(); }); ``` ## What's worth testing Prioritise: business logic (calculations, formatting, validation), edge cases (empty, null, zero, boundary, error paths), and component behaviour (what happens on a click/input). These are where bugs live and where tests pay off. Don't chase coverage on trivial code (plain getters, config objects), don't test the framework or the language, and don't assert on implementation details that change on every refactor. Gut-check: temporarily break a line of production code. If no test fails, that line isn't *meaningfully* covered — regardless of what the coverage report says. ## You've completed the track Fundamentals → Jest basics → matchers → mocking → async → DOM → organization → coverage. You can now write fast, isolated, meaningful unit tests for front-end logic and components — and know when to reach for integration or E2E instead.

text:
Test a real component — no project setup. Jest + jsdom are already wired up on a branch.

git fetch && git checkout assignment/unit-testing-accordion
cd ui.frontend/src/main/frontend && npm install && npm test

```
